stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \ldots, [[n]]\} \) be given the discrete topology, in which every subset of \( X \) is declared open. How many open subsets does \( X \) have (counting \( \varnothing \) and \( X \) itself)?
solution: In the discrete topology every one of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) subsets of a set with [[n]] elements is open, so there are \( 2^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) open sets.
distractor_rationales: This counts pairs/relationships between elements, not subsets.
This counts orderings (permutations) of the elements, not subsets.
This only doubles the element count instead of counting all subsets.
This excludes one subset (such as \(X\) or \(\varnothing\)) that should still be counted.
This overcounts by one extra subset.
This only counts the subsets of a set with one fewer element.

stem: Let \( X = \{0, 1, \ldots, [[nm1]]\} \) carry the usual metric \( d(x,y) = |x-y| \), and consider the open ball \( B([[c]], [[r]]) = \{x \in X : |x - [[c]]| < [[r]]\} \). How many elements does \( B([[c]], [[r]]) \) contain?
solution: The open ball consists of the integers strictly within [[r]] of [[c]], clipped to \(X\): from \( \max(0, [[c]]-[[r]]+1) \) up to \( \min([[nm1]], [[c]]+[[r]]-1) \), which is \( [[answer]] \) integers.
distractor_rationales: This is the naive \(2r+1\) span, which ignores that the ball may be clipped by the edges of \(X\).
This is an off-by-one variant of the naive span that also ignores clipping by the edges of \(X\).
This uses \(\le\) instead of \(<\), so it counts the closed ball rather than the open ball.
This is just the radius, not the number of points inside the ball.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: Let \( X = \{0, 1, \ldots, [[nm1]]\} \) carry the usual metric \( d(x,y) = |x-y| \), and consider the CLOSED ball \( \overline{B}([[c]], [[r]]) = \{x \in X : |x - [[c]]| \le [[r]]\} \), which includes its boundary points. How many elements does \( \overline{B}([[c]], [[r]]) \) contain?
solution: The closed ball keeps every integer within [[r]] of [[c]], including the boundary points at distance exactly [[r]] when they lie in \(X\): from \( \max(0,[[c]]-[[r]]) \) to \( \min([[nm1]], [[c]]+[[r]]) \), which is \( [[answer]] \) integers.
distractor_rationales: This is the naive \(2r+1\) span, ignoring that the ball may be clipped by the edges of \(X\).
This uses \(<\) instead of \(\le\), so it counts the OPEN ball instead of the closed ball, dropping the boundary points.
This is the naive span without the \(+1\) for the center point.
This is only one more than the radius, far too small in general.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \ldots, [[n]]\} \). Define a topology on \( X \) whose open sets are exactly \( \varnothing \) together with every initial segment \( \{1\}, \{1,2\}, \ldots, \{1,2,\ldots,[[n]]\} = X \) (this nested chain is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections, so it is a valid topology). How many open sets does this topology have?
solution: There are [[n]] nonempty initial segments \( \{1\},\{1,2\},\ldots,X \), plus \( \varnothing \) itself, giving \( [[n]]+1 = [[answer]] \) open sets.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to count \( \varnothing \) as one of the open sets.
This double-counts the open sets.
This is a triangular-number count, unrelated to the number of nested initial segments.
This is the open-set count of the DISCRETE topology on \(X\), a completely different (much finer) topology.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
